content.problemStatement:
You are given an array AAA containing N (4≤N≤2⋅105)N \ (4 \leq N \leq 2 \cdot 10^5)N (4≤N≤2⋅105) positive integers.

Check whether there exist 444 **distinct** indices i,j,k,i,j,k,i,j,k, and lll such that:


1≤i<j<k<l≤N1 \leq i < j < k < l \leq N1≤i<j<k<l≤N
Ai+Aj≠Ak+AlA_i + A_j \neq A_k + A_lAi​+Aj​=Ak​+Al​

Input Format

The first line of input contains a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of two lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains a positive integer NNN, the length of the array AAA.
The second line of each test case contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1,A_2,\ldots,A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​ representing the array AAA.




Output Format
For each test case, output `YES` if there exist 444 such indices. Otherwise print `NO`.


You may print each character of the string in uppercase or lowercase (for example, the strings `YES`, `yEs`, `yes`, and `yeS` will all be treated as identical).
constraints: Sum of N over all test cases ≤ 2·10^5
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines: the first line contains an integer N (4 ≤ N ≤ 2·10^5), the length of the array. The second line contains N space‑separated positive integers A1 … AN.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing "YES" if there exist four distinct indices i < j < k < l such that Ai + Aj ≠ Ak + Al, otherwise output "NO".

sampleTestCases.explanation: Test case 1: N=4, 3+4=7 and 2+5=7, sums equal → NO.\nTest case 2: N=5, all elements equal, every pair sum is 2, cannot find unequal sums → NO.\nTest case 3: N=4, 1+2=3, 3+4=7, sums differ → YES.

hidden.label: edge case N=4 equal sums but not all equal

solution.approach:
The condition fails only in two very specific situations:
1. N = 4 and the only possible quadruple (1,2,3,4) gives equal sums, i.e., A1 + A2 = A3 + A4.
2. All array elements are identical. In this case every pair sum is the same, so any choice of four indices yields equal sums.
For N ≥ 5, if the array is not constant we can always pick the first two elements as (i,j) and the next two distinct‑value elements as (k,l); their sums differ. Hence the algorithm is:
- If N == 4, check whether A[0] + A[1] != A[2] + A[3].
- Else (N ≥ 5), check whether the array contains at least one element different from A[0]. If yes → YES, otherwise → NO.
The check is O(N) per test case and uses O(1) extra memory.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, total O(ΣN) ≤ 2·10^5.
